Journal.Title: American journal of physiology. Regulatory, integrative and comparative physiology
Journal.ISOAbbreviation: Am J Physiol Regul Integr Comp Physiol

Article.ArticleTitle: Exaggerated systolic blood pressure response to late night low- and high-intensity resistance exercise in healthy young men.

Abstract.AbstractText: Although resistance exercise (RE) is recommended for optimal health outcomes, it induces a marked acute elevation in blood pressure (BP). Cardiovascular functions exhibit diurnal variations. However, the impact of the time of day on the pressor response to RE remains poorly understood. Thus, we investigated the effect of time of day on BP response to dynamic RE. Ten healthy young men performed dynamic low (20% of a 1-repetition maximum [1RM]) and high (70% 1RM)-intensity one-arm preacher curl followed by 2-min post-exercise muscle ischemia [PEMI], which induces the metabolic component of the exercise pressor reflex. The sessions were conducted in the morning (8:00 am), afternoon (4:00 pm), and late night (12:00 am). Systolic BP responses to low- and high-intensity RE were significantly greater during the late night than in the morning or afternoon (low-intensity RE: morning, Δ0.1 ± 7.4 mmHg; evening, Δ0.6 ± 5.1 mmHg; late night, Δ6.7 ± 4.4 mmHg; high-intensity RE: morning, Δ18.2 ± 12.0 mmHg; evening, Δ16.5 ± 15.5 mmHg; and late night, Δ26.1 ±9.7 mmHg, P ≤ 0.038). No significant difference was found in the pressor responses between the morning and afternoon (P ≥ 0.716). No significant effects of time of day on the pressor response to PEMI were observed (morning, Δ27.5 ± 11.7 mmHg; evening, Δ24.5 ± 13.8 mmHg; and late night, Δ29.9 ± 10.9 mmHg, P = 0.318). Our data suggest that the pressor response to dynamic RE increases during the late night period compared to the daytime.
